title: The Rap-Up: An All-Detroit Linkup, Tigray Funk + Skrilla
author: Donald Morrison
summary:
Screenshot via Bruiser Wolf, Payroll Giovanni & Sheefy McFly – “HATER NOT AN OPP”/YouTube

The Rap-Up is the only weekly round-up providing you with the best rap songs you need to hear. Support real, independent music journalism by subscribing to Passion of the Weiss on Patreon.

Donald Morrison is looksmaxxing in ways you never could have even imagined. 

Judah Weston & IsoKeys – “Thug”
full_text:
Screenshot via Bruiser Wolf, Payroll Giovanni & Sheefy McFly – “HATER NOT AN OPP”/YouTube

The Rap-Up is the only weekly round-up providing you with the best rap songs you need to hear. Support real, independent music journalism by subscribing to Passion of the Weiss on Patreon.

Donald Morrison is looksmaxxing in ways you never could have even imagined. 

Judah Weston & IsoKeys – “Thug” + “Cuff”

VIDEO

VIDEO

There’s something truly endearing about kids having fun and catching lightning in a bottle and figuring themselves out in real time. It’s the power of youth: an endless series of tiny epiphanies only fully understood years later.

Judah Watson sounds a bit like Toronto’s beloved idiot savant Smiley, but for people who aren’t so zonked on pills that lyrics stop mattering. There’s a sneaky depth to Watson’s best songs: quick flashes of sincerity or oddly specific details that feel way too personal to be fake, like “I never thought loyalty ended in peace, I never thought crying would end in a hug,” or “we need love, the more that you give the more that I’ll take, I’m a thug.”

His ace-in-the-hole is producer and friend IsoKeys, whose beats beam me straight back to the ethereal SoundCloud era, just run through a new-school polish. The production is minimal, glossy, and wildly impressive for someone this young. I’m not sure Watson’s sentimentality would land half as hard without the instant-classic beat on “Thug,” which sounds like it could’ve been on Kanye’s hard drive in his prime. Watson meets those sonics in a way that feels way more deliberate than he lets on.

It’s clear Watson and his crew are having a blast surfing his rapid rise through the online underground. Being a Watson fan isn’t just about the songs; it’s about keeping up with his social media antics, where the music arrives wrapped in fast-cut, surprisingly considered videos. It makes listening to him feel strangely intimate, like you’re not just a fan: he’s talking directly to the listener.

Weston seems to lean in on a punch-in style that skips tightly written 16s in favor of something looser, like a freestyle you and your friends might yell over in a parked car. However, you can tell his verses are highly considered. He’s obsessed with the hazards of moving too fast while you’re still young: pill-fuzzed love stories, ride-or-die loyalty that borders on naive, a vocabulary that happily includes the word “boner,” and a half-joking mission statement of “we looking forward to death,” the kind of thing every kid who secretly thinks they’re invincible understands a little too well.

Bruiser Wolf, Payroll Giovanni & Sheefy McFly – “HATER NOT AN OPP”

VIDEO

Leave it to rap’s modern-day Iceberg Slim, Detroit slick-talker Bruiser Wolf, to cut through a growing misconception. Not everyone who hates you is an opp. People with no motion will happily declare themselves your enemy just to drag you into their orbit and pretend you’re peers. It’s a jealous mind trick; when the truth is, they’re not even on your radar. “Hater Not An Opp” opens Bruiser’s upcoming record, Push & Paint (out March 13), an eight-track collaboration with Detroit multi-hyphenate Sheefy McFly, who both raps and handles production. Veteran emcee Payroll Giovanni joins the fun with a double-time second verse, snapping, “My boogie man was the raid van, they hit the crib and took everything, cars, TVs and the platinum chains, well not the platinum chains, pops hid that in some kitty litter, when he heard the bang.”

Sideshow – “BANDIDO GENEROSO”

VIDEO

Sideshow’s four-part album, TIGRAY FUNK, is a worn‑down, autobiographical portrait of the Tigray, Ethiopia–born, DMV‑raised rapper broadcasting from the edge of poverty, addiction, and indie rap-fame. Sideshow raps in a muted deadpan over jittery, kaleidoscopic beats that disguise, but never dull, plainspoken accounts of paranoia, substance abuse, violent memories, and messy family history across 32 lean tracks. One early standout, “BANDIDO GENEROSO,” has him floating over an almost Afrobeats-inspired instrumental with unpredictable drums and crooked flows; at one point he says, “I got homies who can’t read, but they count bands up, ayy, ayy, and them ABC’s, police, they tryna bag us, uh, uh, I come from ID’s to throw where I ain’t have nothin’, uh-huh.” The song captures the stark, sublime, and psychologically frayed perspective that runs through all of TIGRAY FUNK.

Skrilla – “Bazin”

VIDEO

Skrilla borrows the flow and concept of his 2024 track “Brudda” for his latest single, once again reimagining how to rap over the haunting production he’s become known for. “Bazin” opens with head-spinning bars: “Bitch, I’m bleedin’, brother, how you bleedin’? Bible out, give me a readin’, third eye open, I see it, I can see it.” Skrilla’s inimitable flow keeps evolving, growing darker as his career moves faster than anyone, including him, could have imagined. It’s refreshing to see him lean into what he does best, instead of, say, remixing “Doot Doot (6 7)” with Natasha Bedingfield for a quick buck.

Blue Pesos – “Whatchamacalit”

VIDEO

Blue Pesos, one half of South Central LA duo OTM, raps like he’s permanently unimpressed with the world but still three steps ahead of it. Coming up under the late Drakeo the Ruler’s Stinc Team umbrella, he helped push “nervous music” into a new era, trading in hyper-local slang, deadpan threats, and punchlines that sound tossed-off until they stick in your head hours later. On his solo work, including the self-titled Blue Pesos project and singles like “3 Stooges,” “Undisputed,” and “Money First,” he stretches that style into something colder and more precise, gliding over skeletal LA beats with a shrug that feels like its own flex. On “Whatchamacalit,” he’s not trying to reinvent the wheel so much as talk slicker than everyone else riding it. It’s Blue Pesos doing what he does best: entering hyper-local beefs (listen for shots thrown at P4K(aka PopKorn), the Baby Stone Gorillas member who was recently arrested for allegedly pimping a minor), and effortlessly out rapping most of LA.
